label: Newsletter topic
placeholder: What is this newsletter issue about?
helper: Describe the main subject, update, story, offer, or theme.
why: The model needs a clear subject around which to build the entire issue.

label: Primary goal
placeholder: Select the main outcome
helper: Choose the single most important result this issue should achieve.
why: The goal determines emphasis, pacing, CTA strength, and content hierarchy.

label: Target readers
placeholder: Who will receive this newsletter, and what do they care about?
helper: Describe the readers, their context, and their level of familiarity with the topic.
why: Audience context materially changes vocabulary, examples, depth, and positioning.

label: Main takeaway
placeholder: What should readers understand or remember after reading?
helper: State the central idea in one or two clear sentences.
why: A defined takeaway prevents the newsletter from becoming unfocused.

label: Facts to include
placeholder: List the names, dates, details, prices, results, or points that must be accurate.
helper: Include only information that may safely appear as factual content.
why: The newsletter cannot safely infer specific factual details about the user’s subject.

label: Output language
placeholder: For example: English, Ukrainian, Polish
helper: Specify the language for all generated newsletter content.
why: The language cannot always be inferred from the topic or source material.

label: Newsletter format
placeholder: Choose the closest format
helper: This controls the issue’s native editorial structure.
why: Different newsletter formats require different section structures and levels of promotion.

label: Sender name
placeholder: Name of the person or team sending the newsletter
helper: Used naturally in the introduction or sign-off when appropriate.
why: Sender identity affects voice, perspective, and sign-off wording.

label: Publication or brand
placeholder: Newsletter, company, project, or publication name
helper: Leave blank when the newsletter is sent only under a personal name.
why: The publication identity may need to appear in the opening, footer, or sender context.

label: Primary reader action
placeholder: For example: Read the guide, register, reply, or visit the product page
helper: Use Auto to let the model choose one proportional next step.
why: The desired action determines the newsletter’s conversion path and closing.

label: Newsletter length
placeholder: Select the preferred depth
helper: The final length may adjust slightly to fit the supplied information.
why: Newsletter depth materially affects structure and the amount of supporting context.

label: Source material
placeholder: Paste notes, drafts, research, transcripts, announcements, or source excerpts.
helper: The model should use this material without inventing unsupported additions.
why: Raw material helps preserve supplied facts, terminology, and original insights.

label: Requested sections
placeholder: For example: introduction, product update, three links, quick tip, closing note
helper: Leave blank for a structure selected automatically from the newsletter format.
why: Some publishers have recurring sections that must remain consistent between issues.

label: Opening approach
placeholder: Choose how the issue should begin
helper: The opening should still remain natural and relevant to the topic.
why: The opening approach changes the reader’s entry point without requiring a full writing brief.

label: Supporting links
placeholder: Add relevant URLs with a short note explaining each destination.
helper: Only supplied links may be included; the model must not invent URLs.
why: Curated resources and secondary references require exact user-supplied destinations.

label: Dates and timing
placeholder: Add confirmed launch dates, event times, deadlines, availability, or time zones.
helper: Leave blank when the issue is not time-sensitive.
why: Time-sensitive details cannot be guessed safely and may affect urgency and placement.

label: Issue context
placeholder: Mention the issue number, recurring theme, previous issue, or ongoing series.
helper: Use this to maintain continuity with an established newsletter.
why: Recurring newsletters may require continuity that cannot be inferred from one issue.

label: Content to avoid
placeholder: List unwanted topics, phrases, comparisons, promises, or editorial angles.
helper: Use for project-specific restrictions rather than general writing rules.
why: The user may have brand-specific exclusions that the model cannot predict.

label: Tone
placeholder: Choose the closest tone
helper: The tone will be adapted to the audience and subject.
why: Tone is a meaningful brand preference that cannot always be inferred.

label: Brand voice notes
placeholder: Describe preferred wording, personality, style, or examples of how the brand speaks.
helper: Do not include general rules such as “write naturally” or “proofread.”
why: Specific voice characteristics help the output sound consistent with the sender.

label: Delivery platform
placeholder: Select the publishing service
helper: Used only to adapt formatting and presentation conventions.
why: Different newsletter tools support slightly different formatting conventions.

label: Formatting style
placeholder: Choose the preferred presentation
helper: The model will avoid layouts that are fragile in email clients.
why: Presentation style affects headings, section length, link placement, and visual density.

label: Personalisation level
placeholder: Choose how personally the newsletter should address readers
helper: No recipient names or personal facts will be invented.
why: The preferred relationship between sender and reader changes pronouns and narrative perspective.

label: Preferred sign-off
placeholder: For example: Thanks for reading, See you next week, or The Creatornivo Team
helper: Use Auto for a natural sign-off based on the sender and tone.
why: Established newsletters may use a recognisable closing phrase or signature.

label: Invite reader replies
placeholder: Not applicable
helper: Adds one natural reply question when reader feedback supports the goal.
why: Reply invitations are valuable for some newsletters but inappropriate for others.

label: Primary CTA URL
placeholder: https://example.com/destination
helper: Leave blank when the CTA is to reply, reflect, or take an offline action.
why: The primary conversion destination must be supplied rather than invented.

label: Claims and evidence
placeholder: List approved statistics, results, studies, testimonials, or claims with their support.
helper: Unsupported proof, performance results, and quotations will be omitted.
why: Promotional and factual claims require user-supplied evidence and approval.

label: Commercial disclosures
placeholder: Mention sponsorships, affiliate links, partnerships, gifted products, or paid placements.
helper: The model will add a clear disclosure only when relevant information is supplied.
why: Commercial relationships may require transparent disclosure in the issue.

label: Sensitive topic category
placeholder: Select only when relevant
helper: Used to apply cautious wording and appropriate verification notes.
why: High-stakes subject areas require stronger limitations and verification handling.

label: Relevant jurisdiction
placeholder: For example: United States, European Union, Ukraine, or California
helper: Specify the applicable country, state, or region when rules may differ.
why: Legal, financial, political, employment, and regulated information may depend on location.
